1.text: The ninety-first year had now passed, making it six hundred years since Lehi left Jerusalem. Lachoneus was the chief judge and governor of the land.

Nephi, the son of Helaman, had left the land of Zarahemla, putting his oldest son Nephi in charge of the brass plates and all the sacred records that had been kept since Lehi's departure from Jerusalem.
Then he left the land, and no one knows where he went. His son Nephi kept the records in his place — the record of this people.
At the beginning of the ninety-second year, the prophecies of the prophets began to be fulfilled even more. Greater signs and greater miracles were happening among the people.
But some people began to say that the time had passed for Samuel the Lamanite's words to come true.
They began to mock the believers, saying, "The time is past, and Samuel's words have not been fulfilled. Your joy and your faith in this have been pointless."
They caused a great uproar throughout the land, and the people who believed became very sorrowful, fearing that the things that had been spoken might not come to pass.
But the believers watched steadfastly for the day and the night and the day that would be as one day without darkness, so they would know that their faith had not been in vain.
The unbelievers set apart a day when all those who believed would be put to death unless the sign came to pass, the one given by Samuel the prophet.
When Nephi, the son of Nephi, saw the wickedness of his people, his heart was deeply sorrowful.

He went out and bowed himself to the earth and cried out to his God with all his might on behalf of his people — those who were about to be destroyed because of their faith in the tradition of their fathers.
He cried out to the Lord all that day, and then the voice of the Lord came to him, saying:

"Lift up your head and be of good cheer, for the time is near. Tonight the sign will be given, and tomorrow I come into the world, to show the world that I will fulfill everything I have caused to be spoken through my holy prophets."

"I come to my own people, to fulfill all things I have made known to the children of men since the foundation of the world, and to do the will of both the Father and the Son — of the Father because of me, and of the Son because of my flesh. And the time is near, and tonight the sign will be given."
The words spoken to Nephi were fulfilled exactly as promised. At sunset, there was no darkness, and the people were astonished because there was no darkness when night came.

Many who had not believed the prophets' words fell to the earth and became as if they were dead, because they knew that the great plan of destruction they had laid against the believers had been stopped — the sign was already happening.

They began to realize that the Son of God would soon appear. People everywhere across the whole earth, from west to east, in the land north and the land south, were so astonished that they fell to the ground.
They knew that the prophets had testified of these things for many years and that the sign was already happening. They began to fear because of their wickedness and unbelief.

There was no darkness at all that night. It was as light as midday. The sun rose the next morning in its normal way, and the people knew it was the day the Lord would be born, because of the sign that had been given.
Everything had come to pass, every detail, exactly as the prophets had said.
A new star also appeared, just as had been foretold.

From that time on, Satan began spreading lies among the people to harden their hearts, so they would not believe in the signs and wonders they had seen. But despite these lies and deceptions, most of the people did believe and were converted to the Lord.
Nephi went among the people, along with many others, baptizing them as they repented, and there was a great forgiveness of sins. The people began again to have peace in the land.

There were no conflicts except for a few who tried to prove from the scriptures that the law of Moses no longer needed to be followed. They were wrong about this, because they had misunderstood the scriptures.

But they were soon converted and realized their mistake, because they were told that the law had not yet been fulfilled and must be fulfilled completely — not one small detail would pass away until it was all fulfilled. So in that same year they came to understand their error and confessed their faults.
And so the ninety-second year passed, bringing glad tidings to the people because of the signs that came to pass, just as the holy prophets had prophesied.

The ninety-third year also passed in peace, except for the Gadianton robbers who lived in the mountains and terrorized the land. Their strongholds and secret hideouts were so well fortified that the people could not overpower them, so they committed many murders and caused great destruction among the people.
In the ninety-fourth year, the Gadianton robbers grew significantly because many Nephite dissenters fled to join them, causing much sorrow among the Nephites who remained.

There was also much sorrow among the Lamanites, because many of their children who grew up and became independent were led away by some Zoramites through lies and flattering words to join the Gadianton robbers.
And so the Lamanites were also troubled, and their faith and righteousness began to decrease because of the wickedness of the rising generation.

2.text: The ninety-fifth year also passed away, and the people began to forget the signs and wonders they had heard about. They became less and less amazed by signs or wonders from heaven, until they became hard in their hearts and blind in their minds, and began to disbelieve everything they had heard and seen —

imagining in their hearts that it had all been done by men and by the power of the devil, to lead the people astray. And so Satan gained control of the hearts of the people again, blinding them and leading them to believe that the doctrine of Christ was foolish and worthless.

The people grew strong in wickedness and terrible sins. They did not believe that any more signs or wonders would be given. Satan went about leading the people's hearts astray, tempting them and causing them to commit great wickedness in the land.
And so the ninety-sixth year passed away, and also the ninety-seventh, the ninety-eighth, and the ninety-ninth.
A hundred years had now passed since the days of Mosiah, who had been king over the Nephites.
Six hundred and nine years had passed since Lehi left Jerusalem.
Nine years had passed since the sign was given, the one the prophets said would mark when Christ came into the world.
The Nephites now counted their time from when the sign was given, or from the coming of Christ. So nine years had passed.
Nephi, who was the father of the Nephi who now kept the records, had not returned to the land of Zarahemla and could not be found anywhere in the land.
The people continued in their wickedness despite the much preaching and prophesying that was sent among them. And so the tenth year also passed, and the eleventh year passed in wickedness as well.

In the thirteenth year, wars and conflicts began throughout all the land. The Gadianton robbers had become so numerous and had killed so many people, destroyed so many cities, and caused so much death and bloodshed throughout the land that it became necessary for all the people, both Nephites and Lamanites, to take up arms against them.

Therefore, all the Lamanites who had been converted to the Lord joined with the Nephites. For the safety of their lives, their women, and their children, they were compelled to take up arms against the Gadianton robbers — and also to defend their rights, their religious freedoms, and their liberty.
Before the thirteenth year ended, the Nephites were threatened with complete destruction because this war had become extremely fierce.
The Lamanites who had united with the Nephites were counted among the Nephites.
Their curse was taken from them, and their skin became white like the Nephites.
Their young men and daughters became very fair, and they were counted among the Nephites and called Nephites. And so the thirteenth year ended.

At the beginning of the fourteenth year, the war between the robbers and the people of Nephi continued and became extremely fierce. Nevertheless, the people of Nephi gained some advantage over the robbers and drove them back out of their lands into the mountains and into their secret hideouts.

And so the fourteenth year ended. In the fifteenth year, the robbers came out again against the people of Nephi. Because of the wickedness and the many conflicts and divisions among the Nephites, the Gadianton robbers gained many advantages over them.
And so the fifteenth year ended, and the people were in a state of great suffering. The sword of destruction hung over them, and they were about to be struck down because of their wickedness.

3.text: In the sixteenth year from the coming of Christ, Lachoneus, the governor of the land, received a letter from the leader and governor of the band of robbers. These were the words written in it:

"Lachoneus, most noble and chief governor of the land — I write this letter to you and give you very great praise for your firmness, and for the firmness of your people, in defending what you believe to be your right and liberty. You stand well, as if you were supported by the hand of a god, in defense of your liberty, your property, and your country, or what you call it."

"It seems a pity to me, most noble Lachoneus, that you should be so foolish and vain as to think you can stand against the many brave men under my command, who even now stand ready in their armor, waiting eagerly for the word to go down and destroy the Nephites."

"Knowing their unbeatable spirit, having proved them in battle, and knowing their everlasting hatred toward you because of the many wrongs you have done to them — if they should come down against you, they would completely destroy you."
"Therefore I have written this letter, sealing it with my own hand, out of concern for your welfare, because of your firmness in what you believe to be right, and your noble spirit in battle."
"I write to you, asking that you surrender to my people your cities, your lands, and your possessions, rather than have them come against you with the sword and bring destruction upon you."
"In other words, surrender to us, join us, learn about our secret works, and become our brothers so that you may be like us — not our slaves, but our brothers and partners in all that we have."

"I swear to you with an oath: if you will do this, you will not be destroyed. But if you will not, I swear with an oath that next month I will command my armies to come down against you, and they will not hold back but will slaughter you until you are wiped out."
"I am Giddianhi, and I am the governor of this secret society of Gadianton, whose society and works I know to be good. They are of ancient origin and have been handed down to us."

"I write this letter to you, Lachoneus, and I hope you will give up your lands and possessions without bloodshed, so that my people may recover their rights and government. They left you because of your wickedness in keeping their rights of government from them. Unless you do this, I will avenge their wrongs. I am Giddianhi."

When Lachoneus received this letter, he was amazed at Giddianhi's boldness in demanding the Nephites' land, and at his threats to avenge the supposed wrongs of people who had only wronged themselves by joining those wicked and horrible robbers.

Now this Lachoneus, the governor, was a just man and could not be frightened by the demands and threats of a robber. So he did not give in to the letter from Giddianhi, the governor of the robbers, but instead called on his people to cry unto the Lord for strength for when the robbers would come against them.
He sent out a proclamation to all the people, telling them to gather together their women, children, flocks, herds, and all their belongings — everything except their land — into one place.

He had fortifications built around them, making them extremely strong. He placed armies of both Nephites and Lamanites — all who were numbered among the Nephites — as guards around them to watch and protect them from the robbers day and night.
He said to them, "As the Lord lives, unless you repent of all your sins and cry unto the Lord, you will not be delivered from the hands of those Gadianton robbers."
The words and prophecies of Lachoneus were so great and powerful that they caused fear to come upon all the people, and they tried their hardest to do what Lachoneus said.
Lachoneus appointed chief captains over all the Nephite armies to command them when the robbers came down from the wilderness against them.
The greatest of all the chief captains and the supreme commander of all the Nephite armies was a man named Gidgiddoni.

It was the custom among the Nephites to appoint as their chief captains (except in times of wickedness) someone who had the spirit of revelation and prophecy. Therefore, this Gidgiddoni was a great prophet among them, as was the chief judge.
The people said to Gidgiddoni, "Pray to the Lord, and let us go up into the mountains and into the wilderness so we can attack the robbers and destroy them in their own territory."

But Gidgiddoni said to them, "The Lord forbids it! If we go up against them, the Lord would deliver us into their hands. Instead, we will prepare ourselves in the center of our lands. We will gather all our armies together and will not go against them, but will wait until they come to us. As the Lord lives, if we do this, he will deliver them into our hands."

In the seventeenth year, near the end of the year, Lachoneus's proclamation had gone throughout all the land. The people took their horses, chariots, cattle, flocks, herds, grain, and all their belongings and marched by the thousands and tens of thousands to the appointed gathering place to defend themselves against their enemies.
The appointed land was the land of Zarahemla and the area between the land of Zarahemla and the land Bountiful, up to the border between Bountiful and the land Desolation.
A great many thousand people called Nephites gathered together in this land. Lachoneus had them gather in the land southward, because of the great curse upon the land northward.

They fortified themselves against their enemies and lived together in one land as one body. They took to heart the words Lachoneus had spoken, repented of all their sins, and prayed to the Lord their God to deliver them when their enemies came against them in battle.
They were deeply sorrowful because of their enemies. Gidgiddoni had them make weapons of war of every kind and outfit themselves with armor, shields, and bucklers, following his instructions.

4.text: Near the end of the eighteenth year, the armies of robbers prepared for battle and began to come down from the hills, mountains, wilderness, strongholds, and secret hideouts. They began to take possession of the lands in both the south and the north, and all the lands that the Nephites had left behind, along with the cities that had been left empty.
But there were no wild animals or game in the lands the Nephites had abandoned, and there was no food for the robbers except in the wilderness.

The robbers could not survive outside the wilderness because of the lack of food. The Nephites had left their lands empty and had gathered all their flocks, herds, and belongings together in one group.

So the robbers had no way to steal food except by attacking the Nephites in open battle. The Nephites, being in one large group with a great number of people, had stored up enough provisions, horses, cattle, and flocks of every kind to last for seven years. During that time they hoped to wipe out the robbers from the land. And so the eighteenth year passed.
In the nineteenth year, Giddianhi realized he had to go to battle against the Nephites, because there was no other way for his people to survive except by stealing, robbing, and killing.
They did not dare spread out across the land to grow crops, because the Nephites might come and kill them. So Giddianhi commanded his armies to go to battle against the Nephites that year.

They came to battle in the sixth month. Great and terrible was the day they came. They were dressed like robbers, with lamb-skins around their waists, and they were dyed in blood. Their heads were shaved and they wore head-plates. The appearance of Giddianhi's armies was great and terrible because of their armor and because they were dyed in blood.
When the Nephite armies saw the appearance of Giddianhi's army, they all fell to the earth and cried out to the Lord their God to spare them and deliver them from the hands of their enemies.
When Giddianhi's armies saw this, they shouted loudly with joy, thinking the Nephites had fallen down in fear because of the terror of their armies.

But they were wrong, because the Nephites did not fear them. The Nephites feared their God and prayed to him for protection. So when Giddianhi's armies rushed at them, they were ready to meet them. In the strength of the Lord they received the attack.
The battle began in the sixth month. It was great and terrible, and the slaughter was so great that there had never been such a great slaughter among all the people of Lehi since he left Jerusalem.
Despite Giddianhi's threats and oaths, the Nephites defeated the robbers and drove them back.

Gidgiddoni commanded his armies to chase them to the borders of the wilderness and not to spare any who fell into their hands along the way. So they pursued and killed the robbers all the way to the borders of the wilderness, carrying out Gidgiddoni's command.
Giddianhi, who had stood and fought boldly, was chased as he fled. Being exhausted from so much fighting, he was overtaken and killed. And that was the end of Giddianhi the robber.
The Nephite armies returned to their place of safety. The nineteenth year passed, and the robbers did not come to battle again. They did not come again in the twentieth year either.

In the twenty-first year, the robbers did not come to battle directly. Instead, they surrounded the people of Nephi on all sides to lay siege to them. They thought that if they could cut the Nephites off from their lands and hem them in on every side, cutting them off from all outside resources, they could force the Nephites to surrender.
They had appointed a new leader named Zemnarihah. It was Zemnarihah who ordered this siege.

But this actually gave the Nephites an advantage. It was impossible for the robbers to maintain the siege long enough to have any effect on the Nephites, because the Nephites had stored up so many provisions.
Meanwhile, the robbers had almost nothing to eat — only meat they could find in the wilderness.
The wild game became scarce in the wilderness, and the robbers were about to starve to death.
The Nephites kept marching out day and night, attacking the robbers' armies and killing them by the thousands and tens of thousands.
Because of this, Zemnarihah's people wanted to abandon the siege, due to the great destruction they suffered day and night.
Zemnarihah commanded his people to withdraw from the siege and march to the farthest parts of the land northward.

But Gidgiddoni, aware of their plan and knowing their weakness from lack of food and the heavy losses they had suffered, sent his armies out at night and cut off their escape route, placing his forces in the path of their retreat.
They did this during the night and got ahead of the robbers' march, so that the next morning when the robbers began to move, they were met by the Nephite armies both in front and behind.
The robbers on the south side were also cut off at their escape routes. All of this was done by Gidgiddoni's command.
Many thousands surrendered as prisoners to the Nephites, and the rest were killed.
Their leader, Zemnarihah, was captured and hanged on a tree — on the very top of it — until he was dead. After he was dead, they chopped down the tree and cried out in a loud voice, saying:
"May the Lord preserve his people in righteousness and holiness of heart, that they may cause all who seek to kill them through power and secret combinations to fall, even as this man has fallen."

They rejoiced and cried out again with one voice, saying, "May the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob protect this people in righteousness, as long as they call on the name of their God for protection."
They all broke out together in singing and praising their God for the great thing he had done for them — preserving them from falling into the hands of their enemies.
They cried, "Hosanna to the Most High God!" And they cried, "Blessed be the name of the Lord God Almighty, the Most High God!"

Their hearts were so full of joy that many tears flowed, because of the great goodness of God in delivering them from their enemies. They knew it was because of their repentance and humility that they had been delivered from everlasting destruction.

5.text: Now there was not a single living person among all the Nephites who doubted even a little the words of all the holy prophets who had spoken, because they knew those words had to be fulfilled.

They knew that Christ must have come, because of the many signs that had been given according to the prophets' words. Because of what had already happened, they knew that everything else must also come to pass exactly as had been spoken.
Therefore they abandoned all their sins, their terrible deeds, and their immoral behavior, and served God with all their effort day and night.

When they had taken all the robbers prisoner so that none escaped who were not killed, they put the prisoners in prison and had the word of God preached to them. As many as repented of their sins and made a covenant to murder no more were set free.

But those who would not enter into a covenant and who still had secret murder in their hearts — as many as were found making threats against their fellow people — were condemned and punished according to the law.
And so they put an end to all those wicked, secret, and terrible criminal groups, in which there had been so much wickedness and so many murders committed.
The twenty-second year passed, and the twenty-third, the twenty-fourth, and the twenty-fifth — and so twenty-five years had gone by.

Many things had happened that some would consider great and marvelous, but they cannot all be written in this book. This book cannot contain even a hundredth part of what was done among so many people over twenty-five years.
But there are records that contain a full account of everything this people did. A shorter but true account was given by Nephi.
Therefore I have made my record based on the record of Nephi, which was engraved on the plates called the plates of Nephi.
And I am making this record on plates I have made with my own hands.
My name is Mormon, and I was named after the land of Mormon — the land where Alma first established the church among the people after their transgression.
I am a disciple of Jesus Christ, the Son of God. I have been called by him to declare his word among his people, so they might have everlasting life.

According to the will of God, so that the prayers of those holy ones who have passed on might be fulfilled according to their faith, it has become necessary for me to make a record of these things that have been done —
a small record of what has happened from the time Lehi left Jerusalem down to the present time.
Therefore I make my record from the accounts given by those who came before me, up until the beginning of my day.
And then I make a record of the things I have seen with my own eyes.
I know the record I am making is a fair and true record. Nevertheless, there are many things that, because of the limits of our language, we are unable to write.
And now I end my personal remarks and go on to give my account of things that happened before me.

I am Mormon, a pure descendant of Lehi. I have reason to bless my God and my Savior Jesus Christ, because he brought our fathers out of the land of Jerusalem (and no one knew about it except himself and those he brought out of that land) and because he has given me and my people so much knowledge for the salvation of our souls.
Surely he has blessed the house of Jacob and has been merciful to the descendants of Joseph.
To the extent that the children of Lehi have kept his commandments, he has blessed them and made them prosper according to his word.
And surely he will again bring a remnant of the descendants of Joseph to the knowledge of the Lord their God.
As surely as the Lord lives, he will gather from the four quarters of the earth all the remnant of the descendants of Jacob who are scattered across the whole earth.

As he has made a covenant with all the house of Jacob, so shall the covenant he has made with the house of Jacob be fulfilled in his own due time, restoring all the house of Jacob to the knowledge of the covenant he has made with them.

Then they will know their Redeemer, who is Jesus Christ, the Son of God. Then they will be gathered from the four quarters of the earth to their own lands, from where they have been scattered. As the Lord lives, so shall it be. Amen.

6.text: In the twenty-sixth year, all the Nephite people returned to their own lands — every man with his family, his flocks and herds, his horses and cattle, and everything that belonged to them.

They had not eaten all their provisions, so they took with them everything they had left over — all their grain of every kind, their gold, silver, and precious things — and returned to their own lands and possessions, both in the north and the south.

They gave land to those former robbers who had entered into a covenant to keep the peace, the ones who wanted to be called Lamanites. They gave them land according to their numbers so they could work and support themselves. And so they established peace throughout the land.
They began to prosper and grow again. The twenty-sixth and twenty-seventh years passed, and there was great order in the land. They had formed their laws according to fairness and justice.
There was now nothing in all the land to keep the people from prospering continually, unless they fell into sin.
It was Gidgiddoni and the judge Lachoneus and the other appointed leaders who had established this great peace in the land.
Many new cities were built, and many old cities were repaired.
Many highways were constructed and many roads were made, connecting city to city, land to land, and place to place.
The twenty-eighth year passed, and the people had continual peace.
But in the twenty-ninth year, disputes began among the people. Some people became proud and boastful because of their extremely great riches, even to the point of severely mistreating others.
There were many merchants in the land, and also many lawyers and many officers.

The people began to be separated into classes based on their wealth and their opportunities for education. Some were uneducated because of their poverty, while others received great learning because of their riches.

Some were puffed up with pride, while others were extremely humble. Some returned insults for insults, while others endured insults, persecution, and all kinds of mistreatment without retaliating, and were humble and repentant before God.

And so great inequality developed throughout the land, until the church began to fall apart. By the thirtieth year, the church was broken up throughout the entire land except among a few Lamanites who had been converted to the true faith. They would not leave it, because they were firm, steadfast, and immovable, willing to keep the commandments of the Lord with all diligence.

The cause of this wickedness was Satan, who had great power to stir up the people to do all kinds of sin — puffing them up with pride, tempting them to seek power, authority, riches, and the worthless things of the world.
And so Satan led the hearts of the people to do all kinds of sin. They had enjoyed peace for only a few years.

And so, at the beginning of the thirtieth year — the people having been led around by the temptations of the devil for a long time, wherever he wanted to take them, and doing whatever wickedness he wanted them to do — at the beginning of this thirtieth year, they were in a state of terrible wickedness.
They did not sin out of ignorance, because they knew the will of God — it had been taught to them. They chose to rebel against God on purpose.
Now this was in the days of the son of Lachoneus, who was also named Lachoneus, for he took his father's place and governed the people that year.

Men began to be inspired from heaven and sent to stand among the people throughout the land, preaching and testifying boldly about the sins and wickedness of the people, and testifying about the redemption the Lord would bring for his people — in other words, the resurrection of Christ. They testified boldly of his death and sufferings.

Many of the people were extremely angry at those who testified of these things. Those who were angry were mainly the chief judges, former high priests, and lawyers. All the lawyers were angry with those who testified of these things.
No lawyer, judge, or high priest had the authority to condemn anyone to death unless the governor of the land signed the death sentence.
But many who testified boldly about Christ were taken and secretly put to death by the judges, so that the governor of the land did not learn of their deaths until afterward.
This was against the laws of the land — no one could be put to death without authorization from the governor.
Therefore a complaint was brought to the governor in the land of Zarahemla against the judges who had condemned the prophets of the Lord to death without following the law.
Those judges were arrested and brought before a judge to be tried for the crime they had committed, according to the law the people had established.

But those judges had many friends and relatives. The rest of the lawyers and high priests — almost all of them — gathered together and joined with the relatives of the judges who were being put on trial.
They entered into a covenant with one another — the same kind of covenant that had been used since ancient times, a covenant given and managed by the devil, to fight against all righteousness.

So they joined forces against the people of the Lord and made a covenant to destroy them and to rescue those guilty of murder from the punishment that was about to be carried out according to the law.
They defied the law and the rights of their country. They made a covenant to destroy the governor and set up a king over the land, so the land would no longer be free but would be ruled by kings.

7.text: Now I will show you that they did not succeed in setting up a king over the land. But in that same year — the thirtieth year — they murdered the chief judge on the judgment seat.
The people divided against one another and separated into tribes, each person grouping with his family, relatives, and friends. And so they destroyed the government of the land.
Every tribe appointed a chief or leader over them, and so they became tribes with leaders of tribes.
There was no man among them who did not have a large family and many relatives and friends, so the tribes became extremely large.
All of this was done, and there were no wars among them yet. All this wickedness had come upon the people because they had given themselves over to the power of Satan.
The government structure had been destroyed because of the secret alliances among the friends and relatives of those who murdered the prophets.
They caused such great conflict in the land that even the more righteous part of the people had nearly all become wicked. There were only a few righteous people left among them.
In just six years, most of the people had turned away from their righteousness, like a dog returning to its vomit, or a pig returning to wallow in the mud.
This secret group, which had brought so much wickedness upon the people, gathered together and put at their head a man they called Jacob.
They called him their king, so he became king over this wicked group. He was one of the main leaders who had spoken out against the prophets who testified of Jesus.

They were not as large in number as the tribes of the people who had united together — though each tribe's leaders established their own laws. The tribes were not righteous, but they were united in their hatred of those who had made a covenant to destroy the government.

Therefore Jacob, seeing that his enemies outnumbered his people, commanded them to flee to the farthest northern part of the land and build a kingdom there. He planned to wait until they were joined by more dissenters (he flattered them that many would come) until they were strong enough to fight the tribes. And they did so.
Their march was so quick that they could not be stopped before they were out of the people's reach. And so the thirtieth year ended, and these were the affairs of the people of Nephi.

In the thirty-first year, the people were divided into tribes, each person with his family, relatives, and friends. However, they agreed not to go to war against each other, although they were not united in their laws or government, since each tribe followed the rules of their own chiefs and leaders. But they established strict laws preventing one tribe from trespassing against another, so they had some degree of peace. Nevertheless, their hearts had turned from the Lord their God, and they stoned the prophets and cast them out.

Nephi — having been visited by angels and the voice of the Lord, having seen angels, being an eyewitness, and having been given power to know about the ministry of Christ, and also being an eyewitness to the people's rapid return from righteousness to wickedness and terrible sins —

grieving because of the hardness of their hearts and the blindness of their minds, he went among them in that same year and began to boldly preach repentance and the forgiveness of sins through faith in the Lord Jesus Christ.
He taught them many things, but they cannot all be written, and only part of them would not be enough. So they are not written in this book. Nephi ministered with power and great authority.
The people were angry with him because he had greater power than they did. They could not deny his words, because his faith in the Lord Jesus Christ was so great that angels ministered to him daily.
In the name of Jesus he cast out devils and unclean spirits. He even raised his brother from the dead after his brother had been stoned and killed by the people.
The people saw this and witnessed it, but they were still angry with him because of his power. He performed many more miracles in the sight of the people, in the name of Jesus.

The thirty-first year passed, and only a few were converted to the Lord. But those who were converted did truly tell the people that they had been visited by the power and Spirit of God, which was in Jesus Christ, in whom they believed.

Those who had devils cast out of them and who were healed of their sicknesses and diseases truly showed the people that they had been affected by the Spirit of God and had been healed. They also showed signs and performed some miracles among the people.
The thirty-second year also passed. At the beginning of the thirty-third year, Nephi again cried out to the people and preached repentance and the forgiveness of sins.
Remember that no one who was brought to repentance went without being baptized with water.

Therefore, Nephi ordained men to this ministry, so that all who came to them would be baptized with water as a witness and testimony before God and the people that they had repented and received forgiveness of their sins.
Many were baptized unto repentance at the beginning of that year, and so the greater part of the year passed.

8.text: According to our record — and we know our record is true, because a righteous man kept it, for he truly performed many miracles in the name of Jesus, and no one could perform a miracle in the name of Jesus unless he was completely cleansed from sin —
if there was no mistake made by this man in counting our time, the thirty-third year had passed away.
The people began to watch eagerly for the sign that the prophet Samuel the Lamanite had given — the time when there would be darkness across the face of the land for three days.
There began to be great doubt and arguing among the people, even though so many signs had already been given.
In the thirty-fourth year, in the first month, on the fourth day of the month, a great storm arose — one unlike any that had ever been known in all the land.
There was also a great and terrible windstorm, and there was terrible thunder that shook the whole earth as if it were about to split apart.
There were extremely sharp flashes of lightning, such as had never been known in all the land.
The city of Zarahemla caught fire.
The city of Moroni sank into the depths of the sea, and its people were drowned.
Earth was piled up on top of the city of Moronihah, so that where the city had stood, there was now a great mountain.
There was great and terrible destruction in the land southward.

But there was even greater and more terrible destruction in the land northward. The whole face of the land was changed because of the windstorm, the whirlwinds, the thunder, the lightning, and the extremely great shaking of the whole earth.
The highways were broken up, the flat roads were ruined, and many smooth places became rough.
Many great and well-known cities were sunk, many were burned, and many were shaken until the buildings collapsed, killing the people inside. Those places were left deserted.
Some cities remained, but the damage to them was extremely great, and many people in them were killed.
Some people were carried away by the whirlwind. Where they went, no one knows, except that they were carried away.
The whole face of the earth was changed because of the storms, the thunder, the lightning, and the shaking of the earth.
The rocks were split in two. They were broken up across the whole face of the earth so that they were found in broken pieces, with gaps and cracks all over the face of the land.

When the thunder, lightning, storms, windstorms, and earthquakes finally stopped — for they lasted about three hours, though some said the time was longer; but all these great and terrible things were done in about three hours — then there was darkness upon the face of the land.
Thick darkness covered the entire face of the land, so heavy that the people who had survived could actually feel the vapor of darkness.
There could be no light because of the darkness — neither candles nor torches. Fire could not be started, even with their finest and driest wood, so there was no light at all.
No light could be seen — no fire, no glimmer, no sun, no moon, no stars — because the mists of darkness covering the land were so thick.

The darkness lasted for three days with no light seen at all. There was constant mourning, howling, and weeping among all the people. The groaning of the people was terrible, because of the darkness and the great destruction that had come upon them.

In one place they were heard crying, "If only we had repented before this great and terrible day! Then our brothers and sisters would have been spared and would not have been burned in the great city of Zarahemla."

In another place they were heard crying and mourning, "If only we had repented before this great and terrible day and had not killed and stoned the prophets and cast them out! Then our mothers and our beautiful daughters and our children would have been spared and not buried in the great city of Moronihah." And so the cries of the people were great and terrible.

9.text: A voice was heard among all the people across the entire face of the land, crying:

"Woe, woe, woe to this people! Woe to all the inhabitants of the earth unless they repent! For the devil laughs, and his angels rejoice, because of the death of the fair sons and daughters of my people. It is because of their wickedness and terrible sins that they have fallen!"
"I have burned that great city Zarahemla with fire, along with its people."
"I have caused that great city Moroni to be sunk into the depths of the sea and its people to be drowned."

"I have covered that great city Moronihah with earth, along with its people, to hide their wickedness and terrible sins from my face, so that the blood of the prophets and the saints would no longer cry out to me against them."
"I have caused the city of Gilgal to be sunk and its people to be buried deep in the earth."

"The city of Onihah and its people, the city of Mocum and its people, and the city of Jerusalem and its people — I have caused waters to rise up in their place, to hide their wickedness and terrible sins from my face, so that the blood of the prophets and saints would not cry out to me against them."

"The city of Gadiandi, and the city of Gadiomnah, and the city of Jacob, and the city of Gimgimno — all of these I have caused to be sunk, making hills and valleys where they once stood. Their people I have buried deep in the earth, to hide their wickedness and sins from my face, so that the blood of the prophets and saints would not cry out to me against them."

"That great city Jacobugath, inhabited by the people of king Jacob, I have burned with fire because of their sins and wickedness, which was worse than any wickedness in the whole earth, because of their secret murders and conspiracies. They destroyed the peace of my people and the government of the land. Therefore I burned them to destroy them from my face, so the blood of the prophets and saints would not cry out to me against them."

"The city of Laman, the city of Josh, the city of Gad, and the city of Kishkumen I have burned with fire, along with their people, because of their wickedness in casting out the prophets and stoning those I sent to warn them of their wickedness and terrible sins."

"Because they cast them all out so that there were no righteous people among them, I sent down fire and destroyed them, so their wickedness and terrible sins would be hidden from my face, and so the blood of the prophets and saints I sent among them would not cry out from the ground against them."
"I have caused many great destructions to come upon this land and upon this people because of their wickedness and terrible sins."
"All you who are spared because you were more righteous than they — will you not now return to me, repent of your sins, and be converted, so that I may heal you?"
"Truly I say to you, if you will come to me you will have eternal life. My arm of mercy is stretched out toward you, and whoever will come, I will receive. Blessed are those who come to me."

"I am Jesus Christ, the Son of God. I created the heavens and the earth and all things in them. I was with the Father from the beginning. I am in the Father, and the Father is in me, and in me the Father has glorified his name."
"I came to my own people, and my own people did not receive me. The scriptures about my coming have been fulfilled."

"To all who have received me, I have given the power to become the sons of God. And I will do the same for all who believe in my name, for through me redemption comes, and in me the law of Moses is fulfilled."
"I am the light and the life of the world. I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end."

"You shall no longer offer the shedding of blood as a sacrifice to me. Your animal sacrifices and burnt offerings are to be done away with, for I will not accept any of your sacrifices and burnt offerings."

"Instead, you shall offer me a broken heart and a humble spirit as your sacrifice. Whoever comes to me with a broken heart and a humble spirit, I will baptize with fire and with the Holy Ghost, just as the Lamanites were baptized with fire and with the Holy Ghost at the time of their conversion because of their faith in me, even though they did not realize it."
"I have come into the world to bring redemption to the world — to save the world from sin."

"Therefore, whoever repents and comes to me like a little child, I will receive, for the kingdom of God belongs to such people. For them I have laid down my life and taken it up again. Therefore repent, and come to me, all you people of the earth, and be saved."

10.text: All the people of the land heard these words and witnessed them. After these words were spoken, there was silence in the land for many hours.
The people were so astonished that they stopped crying and mourning for the relatives they had lost. There was silence across the entire land for many hours.
Then a voice came again to the people, and all the people heard and witnessed it, saying:

"O you people of these great cities that have fallen, who are descendants of Jacob — who are of the house of Israel — how often have I gathered you as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, and have nourished you."

"And again, how often I would have gathered you as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings — O you people of the house of Israel who have fallen; O you people of the house of Israel, you who live in Jerusalem, who also have fallen — how often I would have gathered you as a hen gathers her chicks, but you would not."
"O house of Israel whom I have spared, how often I will gather you as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings, if you will repent and return to me with all your heart."
"But if not, O house of Israel, the places where you live will become deserted until the time when the covenant made to your fathers is fulfilled."
After the people heard these words, they began to weep and cry out again because of the loss of their relatives and friends.

And so the three days passed. In the morning, the darkness lifted from the face of the land, the earth stopped trembling, the rocks stopped breaking apart, the terrible groaning sounds stopped, and all the loud noises passed away.

The earth came back together and was stable again. The mourning, weeping, and wailing of the people who had survived came to an end. Their mourning was turned into joy, and their sorrow was turned into praise and thanksgiving to the Lord Jesus Christ, their Redeemer.
And so the scriptures spoken by the prophets were fulfilled.

It was the more righteous part of the people who were saved — those who received the prophets and did not stone them, those who had not shed the blood of the saints. They were the ones who were spared.

They were spared and were not sunk or buried in the earth. They were not drowned in the depths of the sea. They were not burned by fire. They were not crushed to death by falling objects. They were not carried away by the whirlwind. They were not overcome by the thick darkness and smoke.

Now, whoever reads this, let them understand. If you have the scriptures, search them and see if all these deaths and destructions — by fire, by smoke, by storms, by whirlwinds, and by the earth opening up to swallow people — are not the fulfillment of the prophecies of many holy prophets.
I say to you: Yes, many prophets testified of these things at the coming of Christ and were killed because they testified of these things.
The prophet Zenos testified of these things, and Zenock also spoke about them, because they testified specifically about us, the remnant of their descendants.

Our father Jacob also testified about a remnant of the descendants of Joseph. And are we not a remnant of the descendants of Joseph? And the things that testify of us — are they not written on the brass plates that our father Lehi brought out of Jerusalem?

At the end of the thirty-fourth year, I will show you that the people of Nephi who were spared, and the Lamanites who had been spared, received great favors and great blessings poured out upon them. Soon after Christ ascended into heaven, he truly appeared to them —
showing his body to them and ministering to them. An account of his ministry will be given later. Therefore, for now, I end my words.

11.text: A large crowd of the people of Nephi had gathered around the temple in the land Bountiful. They were amazed and talking to each other about the great and marvelous changes that had taken place.
They were also talking about Jesus Christ, about whom a sign had been given concerning his death.

While they were talking with each other, they heard a voice that seemed to come from heaven. They looked around, because they could not understand the voice they heard. It was not a harsh voice, and it was not a loud voice. Yet even though it was a quiet voice, it pierced those who heard it to the very center, so that every part of their body trembled. It pierced them to the very soul and made their hearts burn.
They heard the voice again, but they still could not understand it.
A third time they heard the voice, and they opened their ears to listen. Their eyes looked toward where the sound was coming from, and they gazed steadily toward heaven, where the sound came from.
The third time, they understood the voice they heard. It said to them:
Behold my Beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased, in whom I have glorified my name—hear him.

When they understood, they looked up again toward heaven. They saw a Man descending out of heaven. He was clothed in a white robe, and he came down and stood among them. The eyes of the whole crowd were fixed on him, and they did not dare speak, even to one another, because they did not know what it meant. They thought an angel had appeared to them.
He stretched out his hand and spoke to the people, saying:
Behold, I am Jesus Christ, whom the prophets testified would come into the world.

I am the light and the life of the world. I have drunk from that bitter cup which the Father gave me, and I have glorified the Father by taking upon myself the sins of the world. In this I have followed the will of the Father in all things from the beginning.
When Jesus had spoken these words, the entire crowd fell to the earth, because they remembered that it had been prophesied among them that Christ would appear to them after his ascension into heaven.
The Lord spoke to them, saying:

Arise and come to me, so that you may put your hands into my side and feel the marks of the nails in my hands and in my feet. Then you will know that I am the God of Israel and the God of the whole earth, and that I have been slain for the sins of the world.

The crowd came forward and put their hands into his side and felt the marks of the nails in his hands and feet. They did this one by one until they had all come forward. They saw with their eyes and felt with their hands, and they knew for certain and testified that it was he whom the prophets had written about, the one who would come.
When they had all come forward and witnessed for themselves, they cried out together, saying:
Hosanna! Blessed be the name of the Most High God! And they fell down at the feet of Jesus and worshipped him.
He spoke to Nephi (for Nephi was among the crowd) and commanded him to come forward.
Nephi came forward, bowed himself before the Lord, and kissed his feet.
The Lord commanded him to stand up. He stood up and stood before him.
The Lord said to him: I give you power to baptize this people when I have ascended into heaven again.
The Lord also called others and said the same to them. He gave them power to baptize. And he said to them: This is how you shall baptize, and there shall be no disagreements among you about it.

Truly I say to you, whoever repents of their sins because of your words and desires to be baptized in my name, this is how you shall baptize them: You shall go down and stand in the water, and in my name you shall baptize them.
Now these are the words you shall say, calling them by name:
Having authority given me of Jesus Christ, I baptize you in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost. Amen.
Then you shall lower them into the water and bring them up again out of the water.
In this way you shall baptize in my name. For truly I say to you, the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Ghost are one; and I am in the Father, and the Father in me, and the Father and I are one.

As I have commanded you, so shall you baptize. There shall be no disagreements among you, as there have been before. Neither shall there be disagreements among you about the details of my doctrine, as there have been before.
For truly I say to you, anyone who has the spirit of contention is not of me, but is of the devil, who is the father of contention. He stirs up people's hearts to argue with each other in anger.
This is not my way—to stir up people's hearts with anger against one another. My way is that such things should be done away with.
Truly I say to you, I will teach you my doctrine.

This is my doctrine, the doctrine which the Father has given to me: I testify of the Father, and the Father testifies of me, and the Holy Ghost testifies of the Father and me. I also testify that the Father commands all people, everywhere, to repent and believe in me.
Whoever believes in me and is baptized will be saved. They are the ones who will inherit the kingdom of God.
Whoever does not believe in me and is not baptized will be condemned.

Truly I say to you, this is my doctrine, and I testify of it from the Father. Whoever believes in me also believes in the Father. The Father will testify of me to that person, for he will visit them with fire and with the Holy Ghost.
In this way the Father will testify of me, and the Holy Ghost will testify to that person of the Father and me, for the Father, and I, and the Holy Ghost are one.
Again I say to you: You must repent, become like a little child, and be baptized in my name, or you cannot receive these things.
Again I say to you: You must repent and be baptized in my name and become like a little child, or you cannot inherit the kingdom of God.
Truly I say to you, this is my doctrine, and whoever builds upon this builds upon my rock, and the gates of hell shall not overcome them.

Whoever teaches more or less than this and calls it my doctrine is doing evil and is not built upon my rock. That person builds upon a sandy foundation, and the gates of hell stand open to receive them when the floods come and the winds beat against them.
Therefore, go to this people and proclaim the words I have spoken to the ends of the earth.

12.text: When Jesus had spoken these words to Nephi and to those who had been called (the number who had been called and given power and authority to baptize was twelve), he stretched out his hand to the crowd and called out to them: Blessed are you if you listen to the words of these twelve whom I have chosen from among you to serve you. I have given them power to baptize you with water, and after you are baptized with water, I will baptize you with fire and with the Holy Ghost. Therefore, blessed are you if you believe in me and are baptized, after you have seen me and know that I am.

And even more blessed are those who will believe in your words, because you will testify that you have seen me and know that I am. Yes, blessed are those who believe in your words and humble themselves deeply and are baptized, for they will be visited with fire and with the Holy Ghost, and will receive a forgiveness of their sins.
Yes, blessed are the poor in spirit who come to me, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
And blessed are all those who mourn, for they shall be comforted.
And blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.
And blessed are all those who hunger and thirst after righteousness, for they shall be filled with the Holy Ghost.
And blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy.
And blessed are all the pure in heart, for they shall see God.
And blessed are all the peacemakers, for they shall be called the children of God.
And blessed are all those who are persecuted for my name's sake, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.
And blessed are you when people insult you and persecute you and say all kinds of evil things against you falsely, for my sake.
For you will have great joy and be very glad, because your reward in heaven will be great. In the same way, they persecuted the prophets who came before you.

Truly I say to you, I have made you the salt of the earth. But if the salt loses its flavor, how can the earth be salted? The salt is then good for nothing except to be thrown out and trampled underfoot.
Truly I say to you, I have made you the light of this people. A city set on a hill cannot be hidden.
Do people light a candle and put it under a basket? No—they put it on a candlestick, and it gives light to everyone in the house.
Therefore, let your light shine before this people, so that they may see your good works and glorify your Father who is in heaven.
Do not think that I have come to destroy the law or the prophets. I have not come to destroy, but to fulfill.
For truly I say to you, not one small detail has passed away from the law, but in me it has all been fulfilled.

I have given you the law and the commandments of my Father, so that you will believe in me and repent of your sins, and come to me with a broken heart and a humble spirit. You have the commandments before you, and the law is fulfilled.
Therefore, come to me and be saved. For truly I say to you, unless you keep my commandments, which I have given you at this time, you will not enter into the kingdom of heaven.
You have heard that it was said by those in ancient times, and it is also written, that you shall not kill, and whoever kills shall be in danger of the judgment of God.

But I say to you that whoever is angry with his brother shall be in danger of God's judgment. Whoever says to his brother, 'You worthless one,' shall be in danger of the council. And whoever says, 'You fool,' shall be in danger of hell fire.
Therefore, if you come to me, or want to come to me, and remember that your brother has something against you—
Go to your brother first and make things right with him, and then come to me with all your heart, and I will receive you.
Settle matters quickly with the person who opposes you while you are still together, or at some point he may have you thrown into prison.
Truly I say to you, you will not come out of there until you have paid the very last coin. And while you are in prison, can you pay even one coin? Truly I say to you: No.
It is written by those in ancient times that you shall not commit adultery.
But I say to you that whoever looks at a woman with lust has already committed adultery in his heart.
I give you a commandment: Do not allow any of these things to enter into your heart.
For it is better for you to deny yourselves of these things and take up your cross than to be cast into hell.
It has been written that whoever divorces his wife should give her a certificate of divorce.
But truly I say to you, whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, causes her to commit adultery. And whoever marries a divorced woman commits adultery.
Again, it is written that you shall not break your oaths, but shall keep the promises you have made to the Lord.
But truly I say to you: Do not swear at all—not by heaven, for it is God's throne;
Nor by the earth, for it is his footstool;
Neither shall you swear by your own head, because you cannot make one hair black or white.
Instead, let what you say be simply 'Yes' or 'No.' Anything beyond this comes from evil.
You have heard that it is written: An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth.
But I say to you: Do not resist an evil person. If someone strikes you on your right cheek, turn the other cheek to him also.
If someone sues you and takes your coat, let him have your cloak as well.
And if someone forces you to go one mile, go with him two miles.
Give to the one who asks you, and do not turn away from the one who wants to borrow from you.
It is also written that you shall love your neighbor and hate your enemy.
But I say to you: Love your enemies, bless those who curse you, do good to those who hate you, and pray for those who mistreat you and persecute you.
Then you will be the children of your Father who is in heaven, for he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good.
Therefore, the things that were part of the old law are all fulfilled in me.
Old things are done away with, and all things have become new.
Therefore, I want you to be perfect, even as I am, or as your Father who is in heaven is perfect.

13.text: Truly I say to you, I want you to give to the poor. But be careful not to do your charitable giving in front of others just to be seen by them. Otherwise, you will have no reward from your Father who is in heaven.

Therefore, when you give to the poor, do not announce it like the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, so that people will praise them. Truly I say to you, they already have their reward.
But when you give to the poor, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing.
Let your giving be done in secret, and your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly.

And when you pray, do not be like the hypocrites, for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and on the street corners so that people will see them. Truly I say to you, they already have their reward.
But when you pray, go into your private room, shut the door, and pray to your Father who is in secret. Your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you openly.
When you pray, do not use empty repetitions as the unbelievers do, for they think they will be heard because of their many words.
Do not be like them, for your Father knows what you need before you even ask him.
Pray in this way: Our Father who art in heaven, hallowed be thy name.
Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven.
And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.
And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil.
For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever. Amen.
For if you forgive others their wrongs, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.
But if you do not forgive others their wrongs, your Father will not forgive your wrongs either.
Also, when you fast, do not look gloomy like the hypocrites, for they make their faces look miserable so that people will know they are fasting. Truly I say to you, they already have their reward.
But when you fast, groom your hair and wash your face,
So that people will not know you are fasting—only your Father, who is in secret. And your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you openly.
Do not store up treasures on earth, where moths and rust destroy them, and thieves break in and steal.
Instead, store up treasures in heaven, where moths and rust cannot destroy, and thieves cannot break in and steal.
For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.
The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eye is focused on good, your whole body will be full of light.
But if your eye is focused on evil, your whole body will be full of darkness. If the light that is in you is darkness, how great is that darkness!
No one can serve two masters. Either you will hate one and love the other, or you will be loyal to one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and wealth.

When Jesus had spoken these words, he looked at the twelve he had chosen and said to them: Remember the words I have spoken. You are the ones I have chosen to serve this people. Therefore I say to you: Do not worry about your life—what you will eat or drink—or about your body—what you will wear. Is not life more important than food, and the body more important than clothing?
Look at the birds of the sky. They do not plant or harvest or gather into barns, yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not worth much more than they are?
Which of you by worrying can add even a small amount to your height?
And why do you worry about clothing? Consider the lilies of the field and how they grow. They do not work or spin thread.
Yet I say to you that even Solomon in all his glory was not dressed as beautifully as one of these.
If God clothes the grass of the field, which is here today and thrown into the oven tomorrow, how much more will he clothe you—if you are not of little faith.
Therefore, do not worry, saying, 'What shall we eat?' or 'What shall we drink?' or 'What shall we wear?'
Your heavenly Father knows that you need all these things.
But seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well.
Do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will take care of itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own.

14.text: When Jesus had spoken these words, he turned again to the crowd and spoke to them again, saying: Truly I say to you: Do not judge, so that you will not be judged.
For you will be judged by the same standard you use to judge others, and the measure you give will be the measure you receive.
Why do you notice the speck in your brother's eye, but fail to notice the plank in your own eye?
How can you say to your brother, 'Let me remove the speck from your eye,' when there is a plank in your own eye?
You hypocrite! First remove the plank from your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother's eye.
Do not give what is holy to dogs, and do not throw your pearls before pigs, or they will trample them underfoot and then turn and attack you.
Ask, and it will be given to you. Seek, and you will find. Knock, and the door will be opened to you.
For everyone who asks receives, the one who seeks finds, and to the one who knocks, the door will be opened.
What father among you, if his son asks for bread, would give him a stone?
Or if he asks for a fish, would give him a snake?
If you, then, though you are imperfect, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him!
Therefore, whatever you want others to do for you, do the same for them, for this is the meaning of the law and the prophets.
Enter through the narrow gate, for wide is the gate and broad is the road that leads to destruction, and many enter through it.
But the gate is narrow and the way is difficult that leads to life, and few find it.
Beware of false prophets, who come to you dressed like sheep but inwardly are hungry wolves.
You will know them by their fruits. Do people pick grapes from thornbushes, or figs from thistles?
In the same way, every good tree produces good fruit, but a bad tree produces bad fruit.
A good tree cannot produce bad fruit, and a bad tree cannot produce good fruit.
Every tree that does not produce good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire.
Therefore, by their fruits you will know them.
Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven.
Many will say to me on that day, 'Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in your name, and cast out devils in your name, and do many wonderful works in your name?'
And then I will declare to them: I never knew you. Depart from me, you who practice wickedness.
Therefore, whoever hears these words of mine and obeys them, I will compare to a wise man who built his house upon a rock.
The rain fell, the floods came, and the winds blew and beat against that house, but it did not fall, because it was built on a rock.
But everyone who hears these words of mine and does not obey them will be compared to a foolish man who built his house upon the sand.
The rain fell, the floods came, and the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell—and great was its fall.

15.text: When Jesus had finished these teachings, he looked around at the crowd and said to them: You have heard the things I taught before I ascended to my Father. Whoever remembers these words of mine and obeys them, I will raise up at the last day.

When Jesus had said these words, he noticed that some among them were puzzled and wondering what he meant about the law of Moses. They did not understand what he meant when he said that old things had passed away and all things had become new.
He said to them: Do not be surprised that I told you old things had passed away and all things had become new.
I tell you that the law that was given to Moses is fulfilled.
I am the one who gave the law, and I am the one who made the covenant with my people Israel. The law is fulfilled in me, for I have come to fulfill the law, and so it has come to an end.
I do not destroy the prophets, for everything that has not yet been fulfilled in me will all be fulfilled.
When I said that old things have passed away, I do not destroy what has been spoken about things that are yet to come.
The covenant I have made with my people is not all fulfilled yet, but the law that was given to Moses has come to an end in me.
I am the law and the light. Look to me and endure to the end, and you will live, for I will give eternal life to everyone who endures to the end.
I have given you the commandments; therefore, keep my commandments. This is the meaning of the law and the prophets, for they truly testified of me.
When Jesus had spoken these words, he said to the twelve he had chosen:
You are my disciples, and you are a light to this people, who are a remnant of the house of Joseph.
This is the land of your inheritance, and the Father has given it to you.
The Father never commanded me to tell your brothers at Jerusalem about you.
Neither did the Father ever command me to tell them about the other tribes of the house of Israel, whom the Father has led away out of the land.
The Father only commanded me to tell them this much:
That I have other sheep who are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will hear my voice, and there will be one fold and one shepherd.
Because of their stubbornness and unbelief, they did not understand my words. Therefore I was commanded to say nothing more to them from the Father about this.
But truly I say to you, the Father has commanded me to tell you that you were separated from them because of their wickedness. It is because of their wickedness that they do not know about you.
And truly I say to you, the Father has also separated the other tribes from them, and it is because of their wickedness that they do not know about the other tribes either.

Truly I say to you: You are the ones I spoke of when I said, 'I have other sheep who are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will hear my voice, and there will be one fold and one shepherd.'
The people in Jerusalem did not understand me. They thought I was talking about the Gentiles, because they did not understand that the Gentiles would be converted through their preaching.

They did not understand me when I said the other sheep would hear my voice. They also did not understand that the Gentiles would not hear my voice directly—that I would not appear to them except through the Holy Ghost.
But you have both heard my voice and seen me. You are my sheep, and you are numbered among those whom the Father has given me.

16.text: Truly I say to you, I have other sheep who are not of this land, nor of the land of Jerusalem, nor in any of the surrounding areas where I have been to minister.
Those I am speaking of have not yet heard my voice, and I have never appeared to them.

But I have received a commandment from the Father to go to them. They will hear my voice and be numbered among my sheep, so that there may be one fold and one shepherd. Therefore, I will go and show myself to them.

I command you to write down these teachings after I am gone. If my people at Jerusalem—who have seen me and been with me during my ministry—do not ask the Father in my name so that they may learn about you through the Holy Ghost (and also about the other tribes they do not know about), then these writings you make shall be preserved and shall be brought to the Gentiles. Through the Gentiles, the remnant of their descendants—who will be scattered across the earth because of their unbelief—may be brought to a knowledge of me, their Redeemer.
Then I will gather them from the four corners of the earth, and I will fulfill the covenant the Father made with all the people of the house of Israel.
Blessed are the Gentiles because of their belief in me and in the Holy Ghost, which testifies to them of me and of the Father.

Because of their belief in me, says the Father, and because of your unbelief, O house of Israel, in the latter days the truth will come to the Gentiles, so that these things will be made fully known to them.

But woe, says the Father, to the unbelieving Gentiles—for even though they have come to this land and have scattered my people of the house of Israel, and my people of the house of Israel have been cast out from among them and trampled by them—

Because of the Father's mercies toward the Gentiles, and also because of the Father's judgments upon my people of the house of Israel: truly I say to you that after all this—after I have allowed my people of the house of Israel to be struck down, afflicted, killed, cast out, hated, and made an object of scorn among them—

The Father commands me to say this to you: On the day when the Gentiles sin against my gospel, reject my gospel in its fullness, and become lifted up in pride above all nations and all peoples of the earth; when they become filled with lying, deceit, mischief, hypocrisy, murder, corruption, immorality, and secret wickedness—if they do all these things and reject my gospel in its fullness, the Father says: I will take my gospel from among them.
Then I will remember my covenant with my people, O house of Israel, and I will bring my gospel to them.
I will show you, O house of Israel, that the Gentiles will not have power over you. I will remember my covenant with you, O house of Israel, and you will come to know my gospel in its fullness.
But if the Gentiles will repent and return to me, says the Father, they will be numbered among my people, O house of Israel.
I will not allow my people of the house of Israel to overrun them and trample them, says the Father.

But if the Gentiles will not turn to me and listen to my voice, I will allow my people, O house of Israel, to go among them and trample them. The Gentiles will be like salt that has lost its flavor, which is then good for nothing but to be thrown out and trampled by my people, O house of Israel.
Truly I say to you, the Father has commanded me to give this people this land for their inheritance.
Then the words of the prophet Isaiah will be fulfilled, which say:
Your watchmen will lift up their voices; together they will sing, for they will see eye to eye when the Lord brings Zion back again.
Break out in joy! Sing together, you ruined places of Jerusalem! For the Lord has comforted his people; he has redeemed Jerusalem.
The Lord has revealed his holy power in the sight of all nations, and all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of God.

17.text: When Jesus had spoken these words, he looked around at the crowd and said to them: My time is near.
I can see that you are tired and cannot understand all the words the Father has commanded me to speak to you at this time.
Therefore, go to your homes and think about the things I have said. Ask the Father in my name that you may understand, and prepare your minds for tomorrow, when I will come to you again.
But now I am going to the Father, and I am also going to show myself to the lost tribes of Israel, for they are not lost to the Father—he knows where he has taken them.
When Jesus had said this, he looked around at the crowd again and saw that they were in tears. They gazed at him steadily, as if they wanted to ask him to stay a little longer with them.
He said to them: My heart is filled with compassion for you.

Do you have anyone who is sick among you? Bring them here. Do you have anyone who is lame, blind, crippled, disabled, diseased, withered, deaf, or suffering in any way? Bring them here and I will heal them, for I have compassion for you. My heart is filled with mercy.
I can see that you want me to show you what I did for your brothers and sisters at Jerusalem. I can see that your faith is great enough for me to heal you.

When he had said this, the entire crowd came forward together with their sick, afflicted, lame, blind, mute, and all who were suffering in any way. He healed every single one of them as they were brought to him.

All of them—both those who had been healed and those who were already well—bowed down at his feet and worshipped him. As many as could reach him through the crowd kissed his feet, so that they bathed his feet with their tears.
He commanded that their little children should be brought to him.
So they brought their little children and set them on the ground around him. Jesus stood in the middle, and the crowd made room until all the children had been brought to him.
When all the children had been brought and Jesus stood in the middle of them, he commanded the crowd to kneel down on the ground.
When they had knelt down, Jesus groaned within himself and said: Father, I am troubled because of the wickedness of the people of the house of Israel.
When he had said these words, he himself also knelt on the earth. He prayed to the Father, and the things he prayed cannot be written. The crowd who heard him testified of what they witnessed.
This is what they testified: No eye has ever seen, and no ear has ever heard, such great and marvelous things as we saw and heard Jesus speak to the Father.

No tongue can speak, no person can write, and no heart can imagine such great and marvelous things as we both saw and heard Jesus speak. No one can conceive of the joy that filled our souls when we heard him pray for us to the Father.
When Jesus had finished praying to the Father, he stood up. But the joy of the crowd was so great that they were overcome.
Jesus spoke to them and told them to stand up.
They rose from the earth, and he said to them: Blessed are you because of your faith. And now my joy is full.
When he had said these words, he wept. The crowd witnessed this. Then he took their little children, one by one, and blessed them, and prayed to the Father for them.
When he had finished, he wept again.
He spoke to the crowd and said: Look at your little ones.

As they looked, they lifted their eyes toward heaven. They saw the heavens open, and they saw angels descending out of heaven, surrounded by what appeared to be fire. The angels came down and encircled the little children. The children were surrounded by fire, and the angels ministered to them.

The crowd saw and heard and testified of these things. They know that their record is true, for every single one of them saw and heard it for themselves. They numbered about two thousand five hundred people, including men, women, and children.

18.text: Jesus commanded his disciples to bring him some bread and wine.
While they were gone for the bread and wine, he commanded the crowd to sit down on the ground.
When the disciples returned with bread and wine, he took the bread, broke it, and blessed it. He gave it to the disciples and commanded them to eat.
When they had eaten and were filled, he commanded them to give some to the crowd.

When the crowd had eaten and were filled, he said to the disciples: One among you shall be ordained, and I will give him power to break bread, bless it, and give it to the people of my church—to all who believe and are baptized in my name.
